query: is selenocysteine considered an amino acid in biochemical classification

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Essential Amino Acids -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Besides 20 amino acids that participate in protein synthesis, recently, 2 more new amino acids have been described—selenocysteine and pyrrolysine. Selenocysteine occurs at the active site of several enzymes, including thioredoxin reductase and glutathione peroxidase. Pyrrolysine is not present in humans but is used in the biosynthesis of proteins in some methanogenic species, such as archaea and bacteria. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Molecular Level -- Selenium
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium is present in nature in both organic and inorganic forms. The main organic forms of selenium are selenomethionine and selenocysteine. The inorganic forms of selenium are selenite (SeO32-), selenide (Se2−), selenate (SeO42-), and the selenium element (Se). [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Essential Amino Acids -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 20,000 unique protein-encoding genes are responsible for more than 100,000 unique proteins in the human body. Although hundreds of amino acids are found in nature, only about 20 are necessary to synthesize all the proteins in the human body and most other life forms. These 20 amino acids are all L-isomer, α-amino acids. All amino acids, except for glycine, contain a chiral αcarbon. All these amino acids are L-isomers with an R-absolute configuration except for glycine and cysteine. Glycine lacks a chiral center, whereas cysteine has an S-absolute configuration due to its sulfur-containing R-group. Selenocysteine and pyrrolysine are considered the 21st and 22nd amino acids, respectively. More recently discovered amino acids may be incorporated into protein chains during ribosomal protein synthesis. Although pyrrolysine has biological functionality, humans do not incorporate it into protein synthesis. When translated, these 22 amino acids may also be modified through a post-translational modification to add further diversity in generating proteins. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium exerts its biological functions via molecules called selenoproteins, containing the amino acid selenocysteine. The human genome encodes 25 selenoproteins. [13] Selenium plays a role in forming several biologically significant molecules/molecular families in humans and other mammals. Some of these include: Glutathione peroxidases - this family of enzymes catalyzes the reduction of organic hyperoxides and hydrogen peroxide. [14] Thioredoxin reductase - This enzyme functions to form reduced thioredoxin, which serves as a source of electrons for enzymes that play a role in making DNA from RNA precursors. [15] Deiodinase enzymes - These enzymes activate and deactivate various thyroid hormones and hormonal metabolites. There has been speculation that selenium supplementation can help prevent Hashimoto thyroiditis by reducing thyroid peroxidase antibodies, but data remains equivocal. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Function -- Selenium
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium functions as an essential component of selenoproteins that play major roles in dense against oxidation, thyroid hormone formation, DNA synthesis, reproduction, and fertility. The functions of most selenoproteins are unknown, but the known functions involve participation in antioxidant and anabolic processes. A family of antioxidant enzymes named glutathione peroxidases is dependent upon selenium to function to neutralize hydrogen peroxide and organic hyperoxides in both intracellular and extracellular compartments. Deiodinases are a group of three selenoenzymes responsible for converting T4 to T3 in thyroid hormone activation. Selenoprotein-P is the most abundant selenoprotein found in plasma and plays a major role in the transport and homeostasis of selenium in tissues. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Amino Acid Synthesis and Degradation -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The amino acids with polar uncharged R groups are serine (S, Ser, HO-CH 2 -CH(NH 2 )-COOH), threonine (T, Thr, CH 3 -CH(OH)-CH(NH 2 )-COOH), cysteine (C, Cys, HS-CH 2 -CH(NH 2 )-COOH), proline (P, Pro, NH-(CH 2 ) 3 -CH-COOH), asparagine (N, Asn, H 2 N-CO-CH 2 -CH(NH 2 )-COOH) and glutamine (Q, Gln, H 2 N-CO-(CH 2 ) 2 -CH(NH 2 )-COOH). The side chains of these amino acids possess functional spectrum groups. Most have 1 or more atoms, such as oxygen, nitrogen, or sulfur, with electron pairs, allowing hydrogen bonding to water or other molecules. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Cellular Level -- Selenium
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of selenium absorption is not well known. Selenium absorption occurs in the small intestine via mechanisms dependant upon the form of selenium. The absorption of inorganic selenate occurs through active transport with a sodium pump. The absorption of inorganic selenite occurs via passive diffusion. Organic selenomethionine and selenocysteine are absorbed via an active transport mechanism similar to that of neutral amino acids like methionine. Selenium is absorbed into the portal bloodstream from the enterocyte and is transported to the liver in multiple forms. Selenite is taken up by erythrocytes and reduced by glutathione reductase to selenide, which is transported in the plasma bound to albumin. Selenium can also be transported in the form of selenoprotein P. Sometimes, selenium may bind to LDL and VLDL. Selenium is stored in tissues in the form of selenomethionine with variable densities in the liver, muscle, kidney, plasma, and other organs. Selenium excretion via urine in the form of methylselenol. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antioxidants -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The term “antioxidant” is not always clearly defined in either popular or scientific literature. In the most general sense, a natural or synthetic antioxidant directly or indirectly functions to minimize damage to biomolecules (mostly proteins, lipids, and DNA) caused by reactive oxygen species (ROS) and/or reactive nitrogen oxide species (RNOS). Screening complex mixtures of organic molecules (e.g., a fruit juice) for their in vitro antioxidant capacities is popular, but the health-related significance of such measurements is questionable. An “antioxidant nutrient” can be either a precursor or cofactor for an antioxidant molecule or can be an antioxidant in its own right. For example, “selenium” is considered an “antioxidant nutrient” but dietary selenium, in the form of selenite or selenate, is not a functional antioxidant: selenite and selenate must convert to L-selenocysteine which can then get incorporated into glutathione peroxidase (GPX) which is a key antioxidant selenoenzyme. Gamma-tocopherol which is the primary dietary form of vitamin E is both an antioxidant nutrient as well as a functional antioxidant. This article will focus on physiologically significant antioxidants that have been studied either in humans, animal models, or relevant in vitro cellular models. The physiochemical and physiological properties of individual antioxidants are complex, and not all molecules that function as antioxidants are necessarily beneficial to human health. A key goal is to understand how antioxidants modulate acts in signal transduction pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperhomocysteinemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Homocysteine levels are generally categorized into three groups: moderate (16 to 30 micromol/L), intermediate (31 to 100 micromol/L), and severe (over 100 micromol/L). [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium is an essential trace mineral that is vital in maintaining human health. Selenium primarily exists as selenoproteins, which have diverse roles in the body's regular physiological processes and metabolism. [1] In particular, glutathione peroxidase (G-Px), a selenoprotein, contributes to antioxidant defense, safeguarding the body against the damaging effects of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species. Another significant selenium-dependent function involves iodothyronine deiodinases, which facilitate the conversion of inactive thyroxine or tetraiodothyronine (T4) into the active thyroid hormone triiodothyronine (T3). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Amino Acid Synthesis and Degradation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Methionine is converted to S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) by methionine adenosyltransferase. Loss of methionine has correlated with an accumulation of hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2 ) in hair follicles, a decrease in tyrosinase effectiveness, and a gradual loss of the natural hair color. [14] Methionine is crucial for the increase in the intracellular concentration of glutathione (GSH). GSH is an antioxidant found in animals, plants, fungi, bacteria, and archaea. Promoting antioxidant-mediated cell defense and redox regulation is critical in protecting cells against dopamine-induced nigral cell loss by oxidative binding metabolites. [15] Methionine is an amino acid, an intermediate component for the biosynthesis of some amino acids. These amino acids are cysteine, carnitine, taurine, lecithin, and phosphatidylcholine. Methionine is also a medium used in the biosynthesis of additional phospholipids. Improper transformation of methionine can lead to atherosclerosis due to the accumulation of homocysteine. Moreover, this amino acid is essential to reversing the damaging methylation of the glucocorticoid receptor gene caused by repeated stress exposures, with implications for depression. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Essential Amino Acids -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Some amino acids are converted to carbohydrates and are called glucogenic amino acids. Certain amino acids give rise to specialized products. For example, tyrosine can be converted to hormones, such as thyroid hormones, epinephrine, norepinephrine, and melanin. Methionine, in its active form known as S-adenosylmethionine, plays a critical role in cellular processes by transferring the methyl group to various substances through a process called transmethylation. Cystine and methionine are the primary sources of sulfur.

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium displays several different mechanisms of action. Many of the various effects of selenium involve the incorporation of selenium into different proteins to create selenoproteins. [6] There are at least 25 selenoproteins within human tissues that provide different functions in the body. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Nutrition: Micronutrient Intake, Imbalances, and Interventions -- Definition/Introduction -- Minerals
retrieved_docs.content: Molybdenum: Molybdenum is a trace mineral required for the functioning of enzymes, such as sulfite oxidase (responsible for the oxidation of sulfur amino acids), xanthine oxidase (which converts hypoxanthine to xanthine and xanthine to uric acid ), and aldehyde oxidase (essential for phase 1 drug metabolism). Molybdenum sources include beans, grains, and dark leafy vegetables. The molybdenum RDA for adults is 45 mcg/d. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Molecular Level -- Proteins
retrieved_docs.content: Proteins are polymers of amino acids. Amino acids are organic molecules composed of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen. The general structure of an amino acid consists of a central carbon surrounded by hydrogen, an amino group, a carboxylic acid group, and a side chain “R.” Each amino acid has a unique side chain. Nine essential amino acids must come from dietary sources: histidine, isoleucine, leucine, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, threonine, tryptophan, and valine. The body can make eleven non-essential amino acids from precursors: alanine, arginine, asparagine, aspartate, cysteine, glutamate, glutamine, glycine, proline, serine, and tyrosine. There are four structure levels in a protein: primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary. The primary structure of a protein is a chain of amino acids connected by peptide bonds; this determines the subsequent structures and biological functions of the protein. The secondary structure of a protein consists of hydrogen bonding within amino acid chains that create either an α-helix or β-sheet conformation. The tertiary structure of a protein derives from attractions between α-helices and β-sheets of a polypeptide resulting in a three-dimensional arrangement of a protein. The quaternary structure of an amino acid consists of a spatial arrangement of multiple polypeptides in a protein. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Essential Amino Acids -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Proteins are made up of 20 amino acids. Each amino acid has an α-carboxyl group, a primary α-amino group, and a side chain called the R group (see Image. Amino Acid Generic Structure). Unlike other amino acids, proline has a secondary amino group. The side chain varies from 1 amino acid to the other. Nutritionally, amino acids are divided into 3 groups—essential, nonessential, and semi-essential. Semi-essential amino acids are synthesized by the body but are designated essential during periods of stress.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2096
retrieved_docs.content: The concept “Measurement of substance” is linked to the specific test “السيلنيوم (++Se)” through an inverse‑is‑a relationship, indicating that the selenium measurement is a particular type of substance measurement. The selenium measurement itself is further classified by another inverse‑is‑a relationship as belonging to the broader, though undefined, category “كيمياء غير محدد” (unspecified chemistry). This unspecified chemistry category is connected by a has‑parent relationship to the more detailed assay “叶酸+氰钴胺素:印象:时间点:血清/全血:名义型,” which represents a folic acid and cyanocobalamin test evaluated at a specific time point in serum or whole‑blood specimens. Together, these relationships place the selenium measurement within a hierarchical framework of chemical laboratory testing, linking it from a general measurement of substances to an undefined chemistry group and ultimately to a specific folate‑cobalamin assay context.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a classification framework that incorporates a specific subset of biomarker concepts, including BCR‑ABL fusion proteins, the trace element selenium, sex steroid hormones, and the vitamin thiamine (thiamin). By grouping these diverse molecular and nutritional markers together, the terminology provides a structured reference for researchers and clinicians to identify and discuss biomarkers relevant to disease mechanisms, diagnostic testing, and therapeutic monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2859
retrieved_docs.content: The NCIt Antineoplastic Agent Terminology, a structured classification system for cancer‑fighting drugs, includes the concept of sodium selenite (seleničitan sodný). By incorporating sodium selenite, the terminology acknowledges this selenium‑based compound as relevant to the domain of antineoplastic agents, reflecting its potential or investigated role in cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2851
retrieved_docs.content: The CDISC SDTM Laboratory Test Terminology by Name incorporates the laboratory test concept السيلنيوم (++Se). Within this terminology, السيلنيوم (++Se) is classified as a type of الكيمياء غير محدد, indicating that the selenium test is considered an unspecified chemistry assay. This categorization places the selenium measurement under the broader, non‑specific chemistry category in the CDISC SDTM framework, supporting standardized representation and analysis of laboratory data.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1890
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement is directly linked to السيلنيوم (++Se), indicating that the supplement provides the element selenium, represented by the chemical symbol Se, in a form that is biologically active and intended for nutritional support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1783
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement therapy utilizes the substance selenium, referred to as “selen,” as its active component. The Selenium supplement itself is directly linked to selen, indicating that the supplement’s therapeutic effect derives from this element. Selen is classified as a mineral, belonging to the broader category of minerály. Consequently, the Selenium supplement functions as a mineral‑based therapeutic agent, with its efficacy rooted in the use of selenium within the supplement formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1961
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement contains the element selenium and is classified as a form of sodium selenate, indicating that the supplement provides selenium in the chemical form of sodium selenate, which is relevant for its bioavailability and therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 334
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium is classified as a mineral element and is considered to belong to the broader group of heavy metals. As a mineral, selenium is the specific substance referenced by the term “minerály” as its parent category. Selenium supplements contain selenium, providing this trace element in a supplemental form for nutritional or therapeutic use. Understanding selenium’s classification as a mineral related to heavy metals and its availability through supplements is important for assessing its role in health and for guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 717
retrieved_docs.content: The CDISC SDTM Laboratory Test Terminology, both in its “by Code” and “by Name” versions, incorporates the laboratory test concepts السيلنيوم (++Se) (Selenium) and الإستروجين (Estrogen) as included items in their respective subsets. Both السيلنيوم (++Se) and الإستروجين are classified under the broader category كيمياء غير محدد (unspecified chemistry), indicating that they are considered specific chemical analytes within an undefined chemical class. This unspecified chemistry category itself is a child of the more complex laboratory test concept 叶酸+氰钴胺素:印象:时间点:血清/全血:名义型 (folic acid + cyanocobalamin impression at a specific time point in serum/whole blood, nominal type), situating Selenium and Estrogen within a hierarchical framework of related biochemical measurements. In addition, a Selenium supplement is linked to the السيلنيوم (++Se) concept, reflecting the clinical relevance of Selenium supplementation. The “by Name” subset of the CDISC SDTM terminology also contains a Prostaglandin measurement concept, further illustrating the breadth of analytes covered by this standardized laboratory test terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1976
retrieved_docs.content: The CDISC SDTM Laboratory Test Terminology by Code incorporates the concept “السيلنيوم (++Se)”, and this selenium test is classified as a type of “كيمياء غير محدد” (unspecified chemistry). This relationship indicates that the selenium laboratory measurement is part of a standardized terminology set while being grouped under a broader, non‑specific chemistry category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2723
retrieved_docs.content: السيلنيوم (++Se) is linked to the entity “كيمياء غير محدد” (unspecified chemistry) through an inverse‑isa relationship, indicating that the unspecified chemistry is regarded as a subtype or component within the broader category of selenium (++Se). In addition, “كيمياء غير محدد” has a parent relationship to the complex laboratory entity “叶酸+氰钴胺素:印象:时间点:血清/全血:名义型”, which represents a combined measurement of folic acid and cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12) recorded as an impression at a specific time point in serum or whole‑blood specimens and classified nominally. Together, these relationships suggest that the unspecified chemistry may pertain to a selenium‑related aspect of the folic acid + cyanocobalamin assay, integrating trace element analysis with vitamin status evaluation in clinical laboratory practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1686
retrieved_docs.content: The CDISC SDTM Laboratory Test Terminology, both in its code‑based and name‑based subsets, incorporates the laboratory test concepts “السيلنيوم (++Se)” (selenium) and “الإستروجين” (estrogen). Likewise, the CDISC SEND Terminology also includes these same concepts. Within the hierarchical classification, both “السيلنيوم (++Se)” and “الإستروجين” are identified as subtypes of “كيمياء غير محدد” (unspecified chemistry), indicating that they belong to a broader, non‑specific chemical testing category. This alignment across CDISC SDTM and SEND terminologies ensures consistent representation of selenium and estrogen laboratory measurements within standardized clinical trial data frameworks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2911
retrieved_docs.content: GDC Terminology is a structured medical vocabulary that incorporates a diverse set of concepts, including the synthetic androgen methyltestosteron, the heart‑failure combination therapy sacubitril / valsartan, the selenium compound seleničitan sodný, and the bloodstream infection septicemia (except in labor). By encompassing methyltestosteron, the terminology acknowledges anabolic‑androgenic agents used in hormone therapy; through sacubitril / valsartan, it reflects modern pharmacologic strategies that target both neprilysin inhibition and angiotensin‑II receptor blockade for cardiovascular disease; the inclusion of seleničitan sodný highlights trace‑element compounds that may be used as supplements or in specific therapeutic contexts; and the entry for septicemia (except in labor) delineates systemic bacterial infection while explicitly excluding obstetric‑related cases. Together, these entries demonstrate the breadth of GDC Terminology across endocrinology, cardiology, nutrition, and infectious disease, providing clinicians and researchers with a comprehensive reference for varied aspects of patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement is classified as a member of sodium selenate, indicating that the supplement’s active ingredient is the compound sodium selenate, which provides the essential trace element selenium. Recognizing that selenium supplement belongs to the sodium selenate group helps clinicians understand its chemical composition, potential bioavailability, and safety considerations when using it for nutritional support or therapeutic purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1639

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
